Fluoride fused salts have attracted general interest for use in high-temperature reactors because: (1) fluorine has a very low thermal neutron absorption cross section, (2) fluorides have low vapor pressures at temperatures and compositions of interest, (3) molten fluorides are very resistant to damage by nuclear emissions, and (4) there are no serious corrosion problems between many fluorides and nickel-based structural material.

a fertile material, is the only fluoride of thorium. $^{4}$ The fluorides $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ , $\mathrm{BiF}_{3}$ , $\mathrm{Li}^{7}\mathrm{F}$ , $\mathrm{NaF}$ , $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ , and $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ have sufficiently low thermal neutron absorption cross sections, vapor pressures, and melting points to allow their use as diluents for the $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{ThF}_{4}$ .

However, $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{BiF}_{3}$ are unsuitable because the cations are readily reduced to the metallic state by structural metals such as iron and chromium. $^{5}$ The lower thermal neutron absorption cross section of $\mathrm{Li}^{7}$ as compared with that of sodium allows the design of reactors which have a smaller holdup of fissile material and superior breeding performance. $^{6}$

Fluid salt mixtures containing high concentrations of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ are not regarded as attractive reactor fluids because of significant vapor pressure of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ above $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Molten salt reactor systems which are designed to operate at sufficiently high temperatures that alkali fluoride- $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ solvents containing 30-40 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ can be employed may offer advantages in the future, but present preference must be given to $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ on the basis of sublimation.

Consequently, mixtures containing $\mathrm{Li^{7}F}$ , $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ , $\mathrm{UF_4}$ , and $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ which have liquidus values several hundred degrees below the $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ and $\mathrm{UF_4}$ melting points are the most promising core materials for a fused salt thermal breeder/converter reactor.
Before the determinations of the phase relationships can be made in a quaternary system, the 14 limiting unary, binary, and ternary systems must be understood.
A thermal gradient furnace with a single moving thermocouple<sup>11</sup> is used for equilibration in the temperature range 650-1200°C.
Five other thermal gradient furnaces, operating at a maximum temperature of 900°C, incorporate 18 thermocouples each.
In quenching studies made at temperatures below 900°C, sample tubes are distributed among the five furnaces randomly, to achieve maximum reproducibility among independent temperature readings.
The region of temperature overlap, 650-900°C, is used to monitor the single high-temperature furnace.
In the absence of supercooling effects, the completely separate measurements in the thermal analysis furnaces agree within 5°C with those from the thermal gradient furnaces.

The accuracy of the temperature measurements is limited by the characteristics of the Chromel-Alumel thermocouples used. $^{14}$ The invariant point temperature data are so precise that a standard deviation of 1 or $2^{\circ}$ is obtained.
Spectroscopic analysis indicates less than 0.25 wt % impurities.

Because thorium<sup>15</sup> and uranium fluorides are easily converted to oxides or oxyfluorides at elevated temperatures it was necessary to remove small amounts of water and oxygen as completely as possible from the starting materials.

As such mixtures are heated the water evaporates from the system. 'Trace quantities of oxide impurities are converted to products which have not yet been identified but which are likely to be ammonium "fluometallates."<sup>16,17</sup> Upon further heating the ammonium "fluometallates" and the excess $\mathrm{NH_4F\cdot HF}$ decompose.
They were ground to pass a 100-mesh screen and used in the quenching experiments.

3.3 The System LiF-BeF $_2$
A phase diagram of the system $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2^{22,23}}$ (Fig.
3) has been derived at ORNL from the results of thermal gradient experiments.

Mound Laboratory. $^{24}$ These diagrams are revisions of those published by earlier investigators: $^{25-27}$ Two equilibrium compounds occur in the system LiF-BeF $_2$ , the incongruity melting compound 2LiF·BeF $_2$ and the subsolidus compound LiF·BeF $_2$ .

Unsuccessful attempts have been made by the authors to produce the reported compounds 3LiF·2BeF $_2$ $^{28}$ and LiF·2BeF $_2$ $^{25}$ by devitrification of LiF-BeF $_2$ glass and by solid-state equilibration of mixtures of BeF $_2$ and 2LiF·BeF $_2$ .

Because the special purification techniques described earlier in this report were not used by other investigators $^{25,28}$ reports of the existence of 3LiF·2BeF $_2$ and LiF·2BeF $_2$ should be considered tentative.
The compositions and temperatures of the two invariant points and one upper limit of stability may be found in Table 2.

$\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{BeF}_{4} + \mathrm{BeF}_{2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{LiBeF}_{3}$ proceeds very slowly.

The compound $\mathrm{LiF} \cdot \mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ may be observed to grow slowly into solid mixtures of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ which are held for several days at temperatures just below $280^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The formation of LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ glass which devitrifies slowly also prevents compositions rich in $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ from reaching equilibrium rapidly.

Mixtures of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ containing more than 33.3 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ regularly contain only 2LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ and the low-quartz form of $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ if they are cooled under nonequilibrium conditions.[29,30]

The compositions, liquidus temperatures, and primary phases for mixtures of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ which have been referred to in the ORNL literature as C-74, C-112, and C-132 may be found in Appendix C.
Solubilities of NaF, $^{31}$ RbF, $^{32}$ ZrF $_4$ , $^{33}$ PuF $_3$ , $^{34}$ CeF $_3$ , $^{35}$ HF, $^{36}$ and the noble gases $^{37}$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ solvents have been reported.

The reactions M + HF (M = Fe, Cr, or Ni), $^{38}$ CeF $_3$ + BeO, $^{39}$ and CeF $_3$ + H $_2$ O $^{40}$ in LiF-BeF $_2$ solvents have been investigated, as have the exchange reactions between CeF $_3$ and CeO $_2$ and between HfC and HfF $_4$ . $^{41}$

bilities of $\mathrm{PuF}_3$ , $^{12}$ $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ , $^{55}$ $\mathrm{LaF}_3$ , $^{55}$ and $\mathrm{SmF}_3$ $^{55}$ in $\mathrm{LiF}-\mathrm{BeF}_2-\mathrm{UF}_4$ solvents and the reactions of $\mathrm{BeO}^{56}$ and steam $^{57}$ on these solvents have been investigated.

The exchange of $\mathrm{SmF}_3$ (dissolved) and $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ (solid), $^{58}$ the exchange of Hf in $\mathrm{HfF}_4$ and HfC, $^{59}$ and the effect of $\mathrm{AlF}_3$ $^{58}$ on the solubility of the rare-earth trifluorides in $\mathrm{LiF}-\mathrm{UF}_4-\mathrm{BeF}_2$ molten mixtures have been studied.

In addition the effect of thermal cycling on segregation, $^{60}$ the effect of radiation on static corrosion of graphite and of INOR-8, $^{61}$ graphite permeation, $^{62}$ dehydration, $^{63}$ and purification $^{64}$ have been reported for $\mathrm{LiF}-\mathrm{UF}_4-\mathrm{BeF}_2$ mixtures.

It has been suggested that the uranium concentration in a molten salt reactor might be increased by adding the eutectic mixture of LiF and $\mathrm{UF_4}$ .69 Consequently, phase relationships in the quaternary section between 73 LiF-27 $\mathrm{UF_4}$ and 64.75 LiF-4.15 ThF-3l.1 BeF2 have been investigated.
This join contains the fuel mixture 65 LiF-30 BeF2-4 ThF-1 UF4 (BULT 4-lU), and all the compositions which may be produced by mixing 73 LiF-27 UF4 and 64.75 LiF-4.15 ThF-3l.1 BeF2.
